coredata.dc:title: Photodegradation of oxolinic acid in aquaculture effluents under solar irradiation: is it possible to enhance efficiency by the use of TiO2/carbon quantum dots composites?

coredata.dc:description: Antibiotics, such as oxolinic acid (OXA), in aquaculture effluents contribute to the dissemination of antimicrobial resistance, which makes it urgent to develop efficient and sustainable processes for their removal. Aiming a photocatalytic degradation under solar radiation, different carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were produced in this work through a bottom-up hydrothermal methodology and incorporated into TiO2 by a simple calcination method. A total of thirteen materials were synthesized and tested for OXA photocatalytic removal from synthetic and real matrices. Among them, CQDs produced with citric acid and incorporated into TiO2 at 4% (w/w) (TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w)) were the most efficient photocatalysts, providing an OXA half-life time (t 1/2) decrease of 91%, 79% and 85% in phosphate buffer solution (PBS), synthetic sea salts (SSS) and brackish aquaculture effluent (BAE), respectively. Therefore, the herein synthesized TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) composites have shown to be promising materials for a sustainable solar-driven removal of antibiotics from aquaculture effluents.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antibiotics, such as oxolinic acid (OXA), in aquaculture effluents contribute to the dissemination of antimicrobial resistance, which makes it urgent to develop efficient and sustainable processes for their removal. Aiming a photocatalytic degradation under solar radiation, different carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were produced in this work through a bottom-up hydrothermal methodology and incorporated into TiO2 by a simple calcination method. A total of thirteen materials were synthesized and tested for OXA photocatalytic removal from synthetic and real matrices. Among them, CQDs produced with citric acid and incorporated into TiO2 at 4% (w/w) (TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w)) were the most efficient photocatalysts, providing an OXA half-life time (t 1/2) decrease of 91%, 79% and 85% in phosphate buffer solution (PBS), synthetic sea salts (SSS) and brackish aquaculture effluent (BAE), respectively. Therefore, the herein synthesized TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) composites have shown to be promising materials for a sustainable solar-driven removal of antibiotics from aquaculture effluents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Aquaculture is the animal food-production sector that has grown the most in the past few years and, nowadays, 50% of the consumed fish and sea products come from aquaculture production (FAO, 2020). In order to safeguard such a production, antibiotics are usually applied for both prevention and treatment of bacterial diseases (Choi et al., 2020). The main route of drug administration is through medicated feeds due to low cost and easiness of application, but a substantial fraction of the so-administered antibiotics may not be eaten or absorbed by fish (Rigos and Troisi, 2005; Burka et al., 1997; Seoane et al., 2014). Besides, administered pharmaceuticals are not fully metabolized, but are partially excreted in their original forms, which end up in effluent waters together with the non-consumed antibiotics. The discharge of such effluents contributes to the increase of antimicrobial resistance (AMR), which is considered a major public health issue of this century (UNEP, 2017; WHO, 2020). Among the antibiotics used in aquaculture, quinolones, such as oxolinic acid (OXA) (Lulijwa et al., 2020; NORM/NORM-VET, 2019; Carrizo et al., 2021), have been detected in surface aquatic systems because of their long persistence in natural waters (Prat et al., 2006), being their widespread use in fish farms (Choi et al., 2020; Lai and Lin, 2009; Tamtam et al., 2008) an important source.

For a time now, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have provided promising results on antibiotics' degradation (Louros et al., 2020a, 2020b). AOPs are based on the production and use of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which are strong oxidizing radicals that react with most organic contaminants. The difference between the various AOPs is the way in which these ROS are generated (Cuerda-Correa et al., 2020). Among AOPs, photolytic treatments are based on energy provided by radiation, being sunlight the most available and sustainable source. However, concerning OXA, photodegradation under solar radiation has been shown to play a small role in its removal from waters (Turiel et al., 2005; Pouliquen et al., 2007). Therefore, in order to attain efficiencies that allow for its application as a water treatment, photocatalysis can be a green solution. Photocatalysis can be defined as the speeding up of photo-generated electrons in the presence of a photocatalyst (species absorbing radiation), which neither suffers changes nor is consumed in the reaction. Titanium dioxide (TiO2), which is a photocatalytic semiconductor, has already been successfully used for the removal of antibiotics from wastewater under UV-A radiation (Dimitrakopoulou et al., 2012). Commercial availability, low-cost, photochemical stability, and absence of toxicity, make TiO2 a nearly ideal photocatalyst in water treatments (Pekakis et al., 2006; Elmolla and Chaudhuri, 2010). But it also has some weaknesses, like the low use of sunlight due to the high band gap energy (∼3.2 eV) and the rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes (Garrafa-Gálvez et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2021). The incorporation or doping of TiO2 has shown good results in overcoming these issues, with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) recently gaining great interest (Aliyeva et al., 2019).

CQDs are novel nanocarbons that possess very attractive properties in the design of efficient visible light-driven photocatalysts, including the low cost, solubility in water, excellent electron transfer properties (as electron donors and acceptors), wide spectrum and efficient light absorption (Guo et al., 2017). Other properties, such as size- and wavelength-dependent luminescence emission, good biocompatibility, and resistance to photobleaching, contribute to the interest in the synthesis and application of CQDs in different fields such as biomedicine, optronics, sensors and catalysis (Baker and Baker, 2010; Li et al., 2012; Wang and Hu, 2014). As for photocatalytic applications, the coupling of CQDs with semiconductors can expand the photo-absorption range of the latter and promote separation of photo-induced charge carriers. Therefore, such coupling has been proposed as a strategy to improve the individual performance of CQDs and semiconductors in photodegradation (Guo et al., 2017; Li et al., 2010, 2012; Bozetine et al., 2016; Hazarika and Karak, 2016; Yu and Kwak, 2012; Zhang et al., 2011, 2012).

In this context, the main objectives of this work were: (i) the synthesis and characterization of CQDs and TiO2/CQDs composites by an easy hydrothermal treatment followed by calcination; and (ii) the evaluation of the resulting composites' performance as photocatalysts to increase the photodegradation rate of OXA under solar radiation in different water matrices. Moreover, this work also aimed to contribute to the development of a sustainable solar-driven photocatalytic process for antibiotics removal from brackish aquaculture effluent (BAE).

Fischer Scientific provided OXA (>98%). Ultrapure water was obtained using a Purelab Flex 4 purification water system from Elga (Veolia). A 0.1 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution (PBS; pH 8.6) was prepared using 0.6 g of sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate (Fluka, Biochemika, ≥ 99.5%) and 17.1 g of di-sodium hydrogen phosphate dihydrate (Fluka, Biochemika, ≥ 99%), which were then diluted to 0.001 mol L−1. OXA stock solution (10 mg L−1) was prepared in 0.001 mol L−1 PBS, in 30 g L−1 synthetic sea salts solution (SSS) using Red Sea Salt (Red Sea Europe) and in BAE (details are given within section 2.5. Water matrices). Citric acid monohydrated (Pronalab, ≥ 99.5%), urea (Panreac, ≥ 99.0%), sodium hydroxide (pellets, Akzo Nobel), hydrochloric acid (NormaPur, 37%) and ultrapure water were used to synthesize the two types of CQDs. TiO2, namely titanium(IV) oxide (aeroxide P25), was purchased from Acros Organics and ethanol absolute anhydrous was acquired from Carlo Erba. For high-performance liquid chromatography with a UV–visible detector (HPLC-UV) analysis, methanol (Fisher Scientific, HPLC grade) and formic acid (Sigma-Aldrich, > 98%) were used.

For the synthesis of CQDs, two different methodologies adapted from literature were applied. The first one (Mahmood et al., 2021; Martindale et al., 2015) uses the decomposition of citric acid to synthesize water soluble CQDs. In a typical reaction, citric acid (40.0 g) was put in a Teflon autoclave inside an electric furnace (Memmert) for 40 h at 180 °C. The resulting brown gelatinous matter was dissolved in 75 mL of 5 mol L−1 NaOH solution, and the pH adjusted to 7 with HCl. Finally, the mixture was freeze-dried to obtain the CQDs powder. This type of CQDs was named CQDs-CA. The second methodology (Chen et al., 2017) is based on a hydrothermal process, where citric acid (3.0 g) and urea (3.0 g) were mixed with 10 mL of ultrapure water and placed into a Teflon autoclave for 5 h at 180 °C. After cooling to room temperature, a very dark green solution was obtained. Larger particles were removed by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 30 min, using a centrifuge (SIGMA 4-10), and the remaining solution was freeze-dried to obtain the green CQDs powder named CQDs-CAU. Solutions of 50 g L−1 CQDs were prepared by dissolving 0.50 g of CQDs in 10 mL of ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Incorporation of carbon quantum dots into TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: The methodology for the synthesis of TiO2/CQDs composites was based on a facile hydrothermal-calcination method, as described by Chen et al. (2017) and Zeng et al. (2019). In an alumina crucible with magnetic stirring, 1.0 g of TiO2 powder was dispersed in 30 mL of ethanol. Then, the necessary volume of a 50 g L−1 CQDs solution was added (so to obtain TiO2/CQDs of 4%, 5%, 6% and 8% (w/w)) and the mixture was vaporized at 75 °C in an electric furnace. Finally, the alumina crucible was heated in a muffle furnace (Nüve, model MF110) at 300 °C for 3 h with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 and the obtained TiO2/CQDs composites were labelled according to the w/w (%) and the synthesis methodology of the CQDs, for example TiO2/CQDs-CAU 4% (w/w). For comparison purposes, a batch of each type of CQDs (CQDs-CA and CQDs-CAU) and of TiO2 was subjected to the same calcination process (CQDs or TiO2 dispersed in 30 mL of ethanol, vaporized at 75 °C in an electric furnace followed by at 300 °C for 3 h in a muffle furnace), so to obtain calcined CQDs-CA (CQDs-CAC), calcined CQDs-CAU (CQDs-CAUC) and calcined TiO2 (TiO2C).

The crystallinity of the synthesized photocatalysts was evaluated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Malvern Panalytical Empyrean diffractometer with a Cu anode (K-α 1.54060 Å wavelength) handled at 45 kV and 40 mA in a 2θ range of 3–70°, at 25 °C.

Fourier transform infrared with attenuated total reflectance (FTIR-ATR) spectroscopy was performed to analyse the photocatalysts' surface. Each material was placed onto the diamond ATR window of an Avatar 360 Thermo Nicolet spectrometer and scanned over the range 500–4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 in absorbance mode and expressed as an average of 64 readings.

To obtain both UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra and UV–visible absorbance spectra of the solid synthesized photocatalyst for further band gap determination and photoluminescence study, respectively, a GBC Cintra 303 solid UV–visible spectrophotometer was used, with barium sulphate as reference. A range of 850 to 190 nm with 200 nm min−1 scan speed and a bandwidth of 2 nm was selected. The band gap energy of each TiO2/CQDs composite and of TiO2C was determined through the methodology presented in Makuła et al. (2018). Briefly, the band gap was calculated through the interception of the linear of linear zone of Tauc graphic (
α h v
1 γ
v s
h v ) in the reflectance spectra and the baseline of the sample (linear fit used as an abscissa).

Aqueous solutions of each photocatalyst (20 mg L−1) were prepared by sonication until homogeneity. UV–visible spectra (between 200 and 800 nm) of photocatalysts' solutions were obtained with a Shimadzu UV 2101 PC spectrometer using rectangular quartz cuvettes with an optical path length of 1 cm. Fluorescence spectra using solutions of 20 mg L−1 of photocatalyst were recorded using a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer from Agilent Technologies with fluorescent emission between 350 and 600 nm with the corresponding excitation wavelength between 340 and 460 nm with 20 nm increments.
The microstructure of the TiO2, CQDs and TiO2/CQDs was observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) using a tabletop Hitachi TM400 equipment (Tokyo, Japan) operating at 15 kV.

The water matrix can influence the photodegradation behaviour of organic compounds, which include antibiotics, such as OXA (Louros et al., 2020a). Since OXA is used in both fresh and saltwater aquaculture (Lai and Lin, 2009), matrices simulating fresh and brackish water were prepared using 0.001 mol L−1 PBS and 30 g L−1 SSS, respectively, at pH 8.6, in order to represent both types of aquaculture. Furthermore, BAE was collected from a tank of an aquaculture without any treatment. It is to note that, using the methodology described in section 2.7. Chromatographic analysis, OXA was not detected in the BAE water sample. To perform the photodegradation experiments, OXA solutions (10 mg L−1) were prepared by dissolving 10 mg of OXA in 1 L of each water matrix.

Firstly, the water matrices were characterized by measuring the pH, salinity, conductivity, and total dissolved solids (TDS), using a Multi 3320 m from WTW. Total suspended solids (TSS) and volatile suspended solids (VSS) were gravimetrically determined after filtration/incineration by implementing the Standard Methods protocol 2540 D and E (APHA, 1998). Details about the procedure are depicted in Supplementary Material (SM). The chemical oxygen demand (COD) and its soluble fraction (CODS) were colorimetrically determined before and after vacuum filtration, respectively, following the protocol 5220 D of Standard Methods (APHA, 1998). Details about the procedures may be found within SM. Properties of water matrices used in this work are presented in Table S1 in SM.

A solar radiation simulator Solarbox 1500 (Co.fo.me.gra, Italy), equipped with a xenon arc lamp (1500 W) and UV filters that limit the transmission of light below 290 nm, was used for irradiation. All experiments were performed with a constant irradiation of 55 W m−2 (290–400 nm), which corresponds to 550 W m−2 in the spectral range, according to the manufacturer. Before each photodegradation experiment, the lamp was pre-heated during 15 min at the corresponding level of irradiation. The level of irradiance and temperature was monitored by a multimeter (Co.fo.me.gra, Italy) equipped with a UV 290–400 nm band sensor and a black standard temperature sensor. A parabolic reflection system was used to ensure irradiation uniformity in the chamber, which was kept refrigerated by an air-cooling system.

For the photodegradation experiments, OXA aqueous solutions (40 mL) were irradiated in triplicate using quartz tubes (internal diameter × height = 1.8 × 20 cm). Each set of experiments was accompanied by dark controls under identical conditions except for irradiation (quartz tubes were covered with aluminium foil), which were maintained inside the solar simulator during the same time as the irradiated solutions.

Firstly, the most efficient photocatalysts among the here produced materials were selected by carrying out photodegradation experiments in PBS and SSS using the same concentration (10 mg L−1) of each material. The corresponding mass of each photocatalyst was weighted into the quartz tubes (in triplicate) and, after pouring the OXA aqueous solution (40 mL), the tubes were manually agitated for homogenization. Next, tubes were placed inside the solar radiation simulator, where they were maintained under irradiation during the corresponding pre-set time. Under identical conditions, tubes with OXA solution but without any photocatalyst were also irradiated. After the irradiation, aliquots (1.0 mL) were collected from the replicated irradiated solutions and dark controls, filtered using a syringe filter of PVDF 0.22 μm (Whatman), stored in the dark at 4 °C and analysed within 24 h for OXA concentration (details are given in section 2.7. Chromatographic analyses). The concentration of OXA in irradiated solutions (C) was compared with that in the respective dark controls (C 0) so to infer the percentage of degradation in presence and absence of each photocatalyst. After selecting the most efficient photocatalysts for the degradation of OXA, their respective dosages were optimized. For this purpose, photodegradation experiments were carried out as previously described but using different concentrations of each of the selected photocatalysts.

After the selection of the most efficient photocatalysts and their dosages, photodegradation experiments were carried out in PBS, SSS and BAE in order to determine the photodegradation kinetics of OXA. Experiments were performed as above described, but using the corresponding optimized dosage for each of the selected photocatalysts. Also, OXA photodegradation kinetics in the absence of any photocatalyst was determined under the same conditions. For experiments in the absence of photocatalyst, aliquots (1.0 mL) were collected from the replicated irradiated solutions and dark control at different pre-set irradiation times (t, h). Meanwhile, in the presence of each selected photocatalysts, different quartz tubes (in triplicated) were irradiated during each of the pre-set t (h) so to avoid the effect of any possible photocatalyst withdrawal during the aliquots collection. As before, aliquots of the corresponding irradiated solutions and dark controls were filtered using a syringe filter of PVDF 0.22 μm (Whatman), stored in the dark at 4 °C and analysed within 24 h for OXA concentration. The kinetic experimental curves were obtained by representing C/C
0 versus t (h). GraphPad Prism 8 was used to determine the fittings of experimental data to the pseudo first-order kinetic equation C/C 0 =
e
− k t
, where k is the pseudo first-order degradation rate constant (h−1). Also, OXA half-life times (t 1/2) were calculated as
ln ⁡
( 2 )
k
.

The quantitative analysis of OXA was performed by HPLC-UV using a Waters Alliance 2695 Separations Module equipped with a Waters 2487 Dual Absorbance detector. Separation was carried out in a 150 mm × 4.6 mm i.d. ACE® C18 column-PFP (5 μm particle size) connected to a 4.6 mm i.d. ACE® 5C18 guard column at 25 °C. The mobile phase consisted of methanol:0.1% formic acid, 45:55 (v/v), at a flow rate of 0.9 mL min−1. An injection volume of 20 μL was used and detection was performed at 270 nm. Before use as mobile phase, methanol and 0.1% formic acid aqueous solution were filtered through a 0.2 μm polyamide membrane filter (Whatman). Before injection, all solutions were filtered by a syringe filter of PVDF 0.22 μm (Whatman).

Fig. 1 shows the XRD spectra of the CQDs produced and both TiO2/CQDs 4% (w/w). XRD patterns of the remaining TiO2C and TiO2/CQDs composites spectra are depicted in Fig. S1 and Fig. S2, respectively, presented in SM. It is noted in Fig. 1 (C and F), Fig. S1 and Fig. S2 that the diffraction peaks of TiO2/CQDs composites and TiO2C are intense and sharp, implying a high degree of crystallinity in the material structures. Besides, the high similarity between XRD pattern of TiO2/CQDs composites and TiO2C is evident, indicating that the incorporation of the CQDs in the TiO2 does not significantly alter the crystalline structure and phase of the TiO2. The XRD patterns of both TiO2/CQDs composites and TiO2C (where TiO2 anatase and rutile forms are represented by
and
, respectively) have reflections at 2θ = 25.2°, 37.8°, 47.9° and 55.0°, respectively corresponding to (101), (004), (200), (211), (204) and (220) structures for the anatase form of TiO2; and the XRD patterns reflections at 2θ = 27.4°, 36.0°, 41.2° and 54.2°, respectively corresponding to (110), (101), (111) and (211) structures for the rutile form of TiO2 (Fig. 1 (C and F), Fig. S1 and Fig. S2). These results agree well with the standard pattern (Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards (JCPDS) pattern for anatase TiO2 (card No. 00-021-1272) and rutile TiO2 (card No. 00-021-1276)). Only in TiO2/CQDs-CA composites two extra peaks (2θ = 31.7° and 45.4°) are observed, which correspond to crystalline NaCl (represented as
in Fig. 1 and Fig. S2). However, these are not present in the XRD pattern of CQDs-CA and CQDs-CAC, in Fig. 1 A and Fig. 1 B, respectively. Due to the presence of NaCl in the mixture used to prepare CQDs-CA, the powder of CQDs-CA and CQDs-CAC only present the crystalline phase of NaCl that overlaps any amorphous phase of the CQDs (Hazarika and Karak, 2016; Mahmood et al., 2021). The XRD pattern of CQDs-CAU and CQDs-CAUC are visibly different (Fig. 1 D and 1 E).

In fact, there are no matches with the database for the peaks in the CQDs-CAU XRD spectrum (Fig. 1 D). These peaks indicate the existence of crystalline phases in the photocatalyst that could be caused by the presence of the unreacted precursors (citric acid and urea) in the material. It is also observed a broad peak centred at around 25° that has been related to highly disordered carbon atoms (Hazarika and Karak, 2016). Meanwhile, crystalline phases are not identifiable in CQDs-CAUC (Fig. 1 E), which may indicate that part of the unreacted precursors of CQDs-CAU reacted during the calcination process. Therefore, Fig. 1 E shows that CQDs-CAUC only has amorphous phases with two broad peaks centred around 10° and 26°. The increase of intensity around 26° can be derived from the introduction of more oxygen-containing groups (Dong et al., 2014; Peng et al., 2012). Differently, the XRD spectra of the TiO2/CQDs-CAU composites showed identifiable peaks, as it may be seen in Fig. S2 (D, E and F).
FTIR-ATR spectra obtained for TiO2C, CQDs and TiO2/CQDs composites are depicted in Fig. 2 .

As it may be seen, all spectra present a broad absorption band over the range of 3200–3600 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H and implies the presence of a large number of residual hydroxyl groups (adsorbed water) on the surface of the materials. Furthermore, in some spectra, it is possible to identify a peak around 2355 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching of the –CO bond of CO2 present in the room atmosphere. The FTIR-ATR spectra of CQDs-CA together with CQDs-CAC (Fig. 2 A) and CQDs-CAU together with CQDs-CAUC (Fig. 2 B) evidence the complex chemical composition of the synthesized CQDs. This complexity is especially relevant in the case of CQDs-CAU, which may be due to the incorporation of nitrogen (N) from urea and the presence of unreacted precursors. Comparing the spectra of CQDs-CAC and CQDs-CA, it may be inferred that calcination did not significantly affect the surface groups. This was not the case of CQDs-CAU, which calcination to obtain CQDs-CAUC caused transformations in the material that are reflected in the FTIR-ATR spectra (Fig. 2 B) and confirm the observations previously inferred by XRD results (Fig. 1 D and E). For CQDs-CA and CQDs-CAC (Fig. 2 A), the main difference is the intensity of peaks at 1390 and 1560 cm−1, which are respectively associated with the stretching of CH3 and CC groups (Bozetine et al., 2016; Hazarika and Karak, 2016). The absorption band around 2940 cm−1 is associated with the symmetric stretching of C–H groups (Hazarika and Karak, 2016). Both peaks at 800 and 880 cm−1 were identified as CC bending and C–H bond stretching, respectively.

In CQDs-CAU (Fig. 2 B), it is possible to distinguish the peaks 3170, 3014 and 2886 cm−1, which are normally related to the hydrogen intramolecular bond with CO and/or NO2, the stretch of the C–H bond of alkenes and the C–H bond of primary or secondary CH3 and CH2, respectively. It is also observed a peak at 2355 cm−1 previously associated to the CO bond stretch from the CO2 in the room, which, in the case of CQDs-CAU, could also be related to isocyanates (NCO) functional groups. The peaks at 1560 and 1404 cm−1 in the FTIR-ATR spectrum of CQDs-CAU in Fig. 2 B probably correspond to CC bond bending of alkenes, C–O bond of carboxylic acid bending or O–H bond bending from a carboxylic acid or alcohol, respectively. Meanwhile the peaks 1326, 1260 and 1180 cm−1 in the same figure may correspond to a C–N bond stretching from an aromatic amine or a germinal dimethyl, C–O bond stretching from an aromatic ester or alkyl aryl ether and C–O bond stretching from a tertiary alcohol or ester, respectively. The peak 948 cm−1 can be due to the presence of the epoxy group that, together with the previously mentioned groups, is responsible for the excellent water solubility of the CQDs. The C–H bonds referring to the groups RCH = CH2 of CQDs-CAU can be identified by the peaks 996, 848, 764 and 600 cm−1. The last peak observable in the FTIR-ATR spectrum of CQDs-CAU is 550 cm−1, which is normally associated with the stretching of the C–Cl bond. This bond could be present due to some contamination or impurity of the precursors. It is known that the presence of groups with oxygen and nitrogen in the surface of the CQDs can improve their stability without any surface passivating agents or inorganic additives (Alam et al., 2015; Chandra et al., 2016; De and Karak, 2013; Yin et al., 2013).

Some peaks can be identified in the FTIR-ATR spectrum of CQDs-CAUC (Fig. 2 B) beyond the broad band of the superficial water molecules. Between them, those at 3500, 3160, 3050 cm−1 can be, respectively, associated with N–H stretching of a primary amine, the hydrogen intramolecular bond with CO and/or NO2 and the stretch of the C–H bond of alkenes. While the peaks at 2820, 1580. 1234, 750 cm−1, may be associated with C–H bond stretching from an aldehyde, N–H bending of an amine, C–O bond stretching from an aromatic ester or alkyl aryl ether or a C–N group stretching from an amine or the alkoxy C–O–C stretching and C–H bonds bending from groups RCH = CH2 (Bhaisare et al., 2015), respectively.

The FTIR-ATR spectra of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs composites are shown in Fig. 2C and Fig. 2 D. The bands at approximately 650 cm−1 and 1636 cm−1 that are present in both TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs composites are, respectively, due to the stretching of Ti–O–Ti and Ti–O bonds (Yan et al., 2016). Fig. 2C shows that the two outstanding peaks (namely, at 1390 and 1560 cm−1) present in the spectra of CQDs-CA and CQDs-CAC (Fig. 2 A) are also present in the spectra of all the TiO2/CQDs-CA. Meanwhile, for TiO2/CQDs-CAU (Fig. 2 D), the spectra resemble that of CQDs-CAUC, which agrees with the already referred calcination effects on CQDs-CAU. New groups are visible in the spectra of nanocomposites, which suggest that TiO2 was changed and functionalized by CQDs during the calcination.

The band gap energies determined for TiO2C and each TiO2/CQDs composite are presented in Table S2 and Fig. S3 of SM. As it may be seen, the TiO2C band gap energy (3.15 eV) is in the same order of values corresponding to the anatase and rutile crystalline phases of TiO2 (Woan et al., 2009). As for the produced composites with CQDs-CA, their energy bands are very close and slightly lower than the band gap energy of TiO2C, with TiO2/CQDs-CA 8% (w/w) having the lowest band gap energy (3.02 eV). Although the determined values do not present considerable variation (between 3.10 and 3.02 eV), they show the decrease of the band gap with the increase of the CQDs-CA mass in TiO2. Contrarily, the TiO2/CQDs-CAU presented larger band gaps than TiO2C, being TiO2/CQDs-CAU 8% (w/w) the photocatalyst with the highest band gap energy (3.48 eV). Therefore, the larger the mass of CQDs-CAU, the larger the band gap of TiO2/CQDs-CAU, which could be related to unreacted precursors in CQDs-CAU (as inferred by XRD and FTIR-ATR results).

UV–visible absorption spectra obtained for the photocatalysts under study are displayed in Fig. S4, in SM. In the case of CQDs-CA and CQDs-CAC, UV–visible spectra (Fig. S4 A) evidence a strong absorbance below 400 nm, which may be ascribed to the overlap of n-π* transition of CO and π-π* transition of CC functional groups. This agrees with the FTIR-ATR results that were previously discussed. Such intense absorbance at wavelengths below 400 nm is also evident in the spectrum of CQDs-CAU (Fig. S4 B), which furthermore shows a characteristic absorption peak at 333 nm that is attributable to n–π* transition of CC, CO, and C–N (Mahala et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2013). In the case of CQDs-CAUC (Fig. S4 B), absorbance at wavelengths below 400 nm is quite lower than for the previous CQDs.

The UV–visible spectrum of TiO2C (Fig. S4 C) exhibits a broad absorption band from 190 to 400 nm, indicating the efficient photo-absorption, which is useful to its photocatalytic properties. In fact, an efficient absorption in the visible region is desirable since it implies that the photocatalyst has a good photocatalytic ability under visible light (Bineesh et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2011; Zhu et al., 2015). In the case of the TiO2/CQDs-CA composites, UV–visible spectra are represented together with that of TiO2C in Fig. S4 D, which evidences that all spectra have a similar pattern (TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) showing the largest absorption), even when compared with TiO2C. The same trend may be observed for spectra corresponding to TiO2/CQDs-CAU (Fig. S4 E) (TiO2/CQDs-CAU 4% (w/w) being the most absorbing). For comparison purposes, the UV–visible spectra of TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) and TiO2/CQDs-CAU 4% (w/w) are shown together in Fig. S4 F, which evidences their similarity.

As revealed in Fig. S5 of the SM, the fluorescence spectra of CQDs exhibited the wavelength-dependent nature of carbon dots for emission spectra. Each type of CQDs presented a different fluorescence spectrum. For CQDs-CA, CQDs-CAC and CQDs-CAU, the maximum intensity of emission observed at 455 nm, 430 nm, and 428 nm, respectively, was obtained with an excitation at 340 nm. On the other hand, CQDs-CAUC maximum intensity of emission observed at 438 nm was obtained with an excitation at 360 nm and was shifted to 545 nm with the increase of excitation wavelengths to 460 nm. For all CQDs, as the excitation wavelength increased, the intensity of fluorescence decreased accordingly and shifted to longer wavelength (red shift). And it is worth to note that the fluorescence intensity decreases in reverse order to the excitation wavelength. As claimed by Bhaisare et al. (2015), this phenomenon may be attributed to functional groups and/or defects on the surface of CQDs. The fluorescence intensity also depends on the number of particles excited at a particular wavelength. In most CQDs, the highest fluorescence intensity was observed under excitation of 340 nm since, at that wavelength, the number of excitated particles is maximized. The presence of different types of functional groups on the surface of CQDs may produce different emissive traps between π and π* of C
Image 1 C. The self-passivated nitrogen (in CQDs-CAU and CQDs-CAUC) and oxygen containing functional groups on CQDs surface may be responsible for the efficient photoluminescence (Hazarika and Karak, 2016).

It was also observed that CQDs, TiO2C and TiO2/CQDs demonstrated an excitation wavelength dependent down conversion emission. This is due to the existence of different surface states and size dispersion of the nanomaterials. TiO2C and TiO2/CQDs present similar fluorescence spectrum. The maximum emission peaks for both TiO2C and TiO2/CQDs were shifted from 426 to 486 nm with the increase of excitation wavelengths from 380 to 460 nm in emission spectra. It is possible to verify that the maximum emission peak around 426 nm was reached with excitation at 400 nm. Additionally, as presented in Fig. S5, the intensity of fluorescence of TiO2/CQDs increases with decreasing concentration of CQD. This is due to the fact that increasing interactions among the different polar groups at higher concentration resulted in agglomeration (Hazarika and Karak, 2016).

SEM analysis was used to elucidate the morphology and structural features of TiO2, CQDs and TiO2/CQDs. Fig. 3 presents the SEM images of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs and shows that both TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs have a spherical-like shape crystal structure, with obvious aggregation. Moreover, it is observed that the introduction of CQDs on TiO2 does not change the typical morphology of TiO2. On the other hand, Fig. S6 (presented in SM) shows only slight differences between the CQDs-CAU and the CQDs-CA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of the most efficient photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Results of OXA photolysis (without photocatalyst) and photocatalysis using the different materials here produced in either PBS or SSS are represented in Fig. 4 .

As it may be seen in Fig. 4, even without photocatalyst, the concentration of OXA decreased under irradiation so pointing to the photolysis of the antibiotic, which was more evident in PBS (Fig. 4 A) than in SSS (Fig. 4 B). As for the photocatalysts produced and tested in this work, Fig. 4 shows that not all of them improved the photodegradation of OXA. Furthermore, the efficiency of the tested photocatalysts was different depending on the water matrix, with all the materials being more efficient in PBS (Fig. 4 A) than in SSS (Fig. 4 B). In PBS (Fig. 4), the most pronounced OXA photodegradation occurred under the utilization of TiO2/CQDs-CAU 4% (w/w) followed by TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w), with C/C 0 of 0.31 ± 0.02 and 0.309 ± 0.005, respectively, after 1 h of irradiation. These two composites were the only ones able to surpass the photocatalytic activity of TiO2C (C/C 0 of 0.330 ± 0.002, after 1 h irradiation). Meanwhile, in SSS (Fig. 4 B), all photocatalysts presented poorer results than in PBS, with TiO2C not being able to improve OXA photodegradation in comparison with photolysis. The most efficient photocatalyst in SSS was CQDs-CAU followed by TiO2/CQDs-CAU 4% (w/w) and TiO2/CQDs-CAU 8% (w/w), with C/C 0 of 0.587 ± 0.007, 0.54 ± 0.02 and 0.548 ± 0.006, respectively, after 3 h of irradiation. Differences in OXA photodegradation depending on the water matrix were already observed by Louros et al. (2020a), who showed that both organic matter and salinity have a negative effect on OXA photodegradation. These authors observed that a lower irradiation time was necessary for 50% OXA photodegradation in PBS (1 h) than in SSS (3 h).

On the basis of the obtained results on the photocatalytic degradation of OXA (Fig. 4), TiO2/CQDs-CAU 4% (w/w) and TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) were the selected photocatalysts for further studies in PBS, while TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) and TiO2/CQDs-CAU 8% (w/w) were the selected ones for studies in SSS.

Results using different photocatalysts dosage for OXA photodegradation in PBS and in SSS are presented in Fig. 5 and Fig. 6 , respectively. As it may be seen in these figures, under the conditions here tested, increasing photocatalysts' concentration allowed for higher OXA removal. For OXA in PBS (Fig. 5 A), the removal percentage after 1 h of irradiation was larger under the presence of TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) than TiO2/CQDs-CAU 4% (w/w), the first being selected for the subsequent experiments using higher photocatalyst concentration, with lower irradiation time. Results after 30 and 15 min of irradiation are depicted in Fig. 5 B and Fig. 5 C, respectively. Although the higher concentration of TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) here tested (750 mg L−1) allowed for the highest decrease of OXA concentration after 15 min of irradiation, dark controls allowed to verify that OXA adsorption onto the photocatalyst contributed for 21.4% of the antibiotic removal. Therefore, the concentration of 500 mg L−1 was selected for further studies, providing a total removal of 94.4% (76.6% photodegradation and 17.8% adsorption) in 0.001 mol L−1 PBS after just 15 min of irradiation.

The selected photocatalysts for OXA photodegradation studies in SSS were TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) and TiO2/CQDs-CAU 8% (w/w), which use at different concentrations (5–100 mg L−1) was tested under irradiation during 3 h (Fig. 6 A). Then, higher concentrations of both photocatalysts, between 500 and 1000 mg L−1, were tested under irradiation for 1 h (Fig. 6 B). As it may be seen in Fig. 6 B, 1000 mg L−1 of TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) presented the most efficient photocatalytic activity, with 75.4% of OXA photodegradation after 1 h of irradiation. It must be referred that, in this matrix and using this photocatalyst, adsorption did not contribute for OXA removal, which may be related with the higher water salinity.

Based on the results obtained in the previous section, TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) was used for the OXA photodegradation kinetic experiments at concentrations of 500 mg L−1 in PBS and 1000 mg L−1 in SSS. Furthermore, considering the properties of the BAE and 30 g L−1 of SSS (Table S1, in the SM), a concentration of 1000 mg L−1 of TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) was used for the kinetic study in BAE. The photodegradation kinetic curves of OXA without photocatalyst and with the selected photocatalysts are respectively presented in Fig. 7 .
Kinetic parameters from the fittings of experimental data to the pseudo first-order kinetic equation are given in Table 1 , together with the corresponding t 1/2 (h).

As it may be seen in Fig. 7, OXA photodegradation was faster in PBS than in the saline matrices and, in all matrices, it was accelerated by the presence of TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w). In the absence of photocatalyst, OXA presented the fastest photodegradation rate in PBS (k = (0.779 ± 0.006) h−1) followed by SSS (k = (0.231 ± 0.008) h−1) and BAE (k = (0.187 ± 0.006) h−1) (Table 1). Under the presence of the selected photocatalyst, OXA photodegradation rates increased, with the highest one being in PBS (k = (8.4 ± 0.2) h−1) as well. This was followed by the kinetic rate in BAE (k = (1.23 ± 0.02) h−1), leaving SSS as the matrix with the lowest k in this case (k = (1.09 ± 0.03) h−1), as shown in Table 1. Differences between the kinetics in BAE and SSS could be related to the formation of more stable complexes of OXA with the inorganic matter, such as ions, a in SSS than in BAE, so retarding its photodegradation by the photocatalyst-generated radicals (Rigos et al., 2004). Furthermore, the pH of the BAE is slightly lower than that of 30 g L−1 SSS, which may affect not only the OXA photodegradation (Louros et al., 2020a) as well as the photocatalytic activity of TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w), since several of the groups identified at its surface by FTIR-ATR analysis may be affected by pH.

Regarding the t 1/2 (Table 1), it may be seen that without photocatalyst, OXA has the highest t 1/2 (t 1/2 = (3.7 ± 0.1) h) in BAE, followed by SSS (t 1/2 = (3.0 ± 0.1) h) and PBS (t 1/2 = (0.890 ± 0.007) h), respectively. Comparing these values with the OXA t 1/2 in presence of TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w), it is evident that the latter are significantly shorter (between (0.083 ± 0.002) h, in PBS, and (0.64 ± 0.02) h, in SSS). Comparing with the most recent published data for OXA photocatalysis, these results using TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) seem promising. For instance, Gaeta et al. (2020) obtained 90% of OXA removal after 40 min in ultrapure water (for an OXA concentration of 3 × 10−5 mol L−1 at pH 7.0) using TiO2. On the other hand, Silva et al. (2021) obtained a t 1/2 of (0.46 ± 0.01) h for OXA (20 mg L−1 in PBS, pH 7.3) using biochar-TiO2 magnetic nanocomposites, a higher value compared with the t 1/2 of (0.083 ± 0.002) h here obtained in PBS.

Overall, although the comparison of this work results with others in literature cannot be directly performed (conditions and photocatalysts are different), it is possible to state that the use of the produced TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) is a promising option for the photocatalytic removal of OXA in different water matrices. Still, a main difficulty for the implementation of photocatalytic wastewater treatments using suspended nano-sized photocatalysts is their after-use separation. Therefore, in view of the practical application of the here studied composites, their magnetization is to be assessed so to allow for their magnetic separation from the treated water and their subsequent reutilization.

Different CQDs were synthesized and used for producing TiO2/CQDs composites by simple and easy hydrothermal treatment and calcination-based methodologies. The characterization of the composites showed no big differences between composites and TiO2 in crystalline phases but changes in the surface groups. Meanwhile, photodegradation results demonstrated that, between 4 and 8% (w/w), the lowest percentage of CQDs incorporated in TiO2 allowed for the highest photocatalytic removal of OXA either in PBS or in SSS. Among the produced composites, TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) was the most efficient for the photocatalytic degradation of OXA. The selected dosages of this composite for subsequent kinetic studies were 500 mg L−1 TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) in PBS and 1000 mg L−1 in SSS and BAE. Under these conditions, k was (8.4 ± 0.2), (1.09 ± 0.03) and (1.23 ± 0.02) h−1 in PBS, SSS and BAE, respectively, which meant that OXA t 1/2 was 11, 5 and 7 times shorter, respectively, than in absence of photocatalyst in each of these matrices. Obtained results point to the produced TiO2/CQDs-CA 4% (w/w) composite as a promising photocatalyst for a green, sustainable, and efficient solar-driven treatment aimed at the removal of antibiotics from aquaculture effluents.
